title: A hybrid computational scheme for singularly perturbed Burgers‚Äô-Huxley equation
author: Daba, Gonfa

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: There exists a class of singularly perturbed partial differential equations¬†known as the nonlinear singularly perturbed Burgers‚Äô-Huxley equations¬†(SPBHEs) that consist of nonlinearity and perturbation parameter, say őĶ(0<őĶ‚Č™1) which is multiplied by the highest order derivative. These equations¬†are an extension of the regular Burgers‚Äô equation, which plays an important role in non-linear physics [13]. Due to their significance in a various scientific disciplines, the study of SPBHEs has got great practical interest among scientists. The non-linear SPBHE arises in many phenomena such as bursting oscillation [4], population genetics [1], interspike [18], bifurcation and chaos [23], membrane models based on the dynamics of potassium and sodium ion fluxes [16] and references therein. To investigate the behaviour of the physical phenomena of these problems, it is often vital to find their solution numerically. However, it is very tough to solve non-linear SPBHE analytically due to the presence of the perturbation parameter and the nonlinearity in the problem. As őĶ tends to zero, the classical computational techniques are fail to provide acceptable solutions for such types of problems. So, it is obligatory to develop robust numerical methods that solve the problems under consideration effectively.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: In the literature, regular Burgers‚Äô Huxley equations¬†are well solved in numerous research works, some of which can be referred in [5], [7], [8], [9], [10], [14], [15], [19], [20], [21], [22], [24], [25], [26] for the detail discussions. However, very few parameter uniform numerical schemes are available for non-linear SPBHEs. For instance, Kaushik and Sharma [13] solved SPBHE using finite difference method (FDM). Gupta and Kadalbajoo [6] treated SPBHE by constructing hybrid parameter-uniform numerical schemes on a piecewise uniform Shishkin mesh. A uniformly convergent FDM on an adaptive nonuniform grid for Eq.¬†(1) is presented by Liu et¬†al. [17]. Jima and File, [11], Kabato and Duressa [12] and Derzie et¬†al. [2] suggested a parameter uniform numerical schemes based on fitted operator techniques SPBHE. The use of numerical simulation for the analysis of dynamical systems of non-linear and non-smooth control problems has gained remarkable attention from scientific community. For further information, one can refer [28], [29], [30], [31], [32], [33].

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: In this work, we are motivated to develop and analyse an őĶ‚ąíuniformly convergent numerical scheme for SPBHE. Developing parameter uniform numerical techniques for SPBHE is a desirable task and active for research. A detailed description of the formulation of the scheme and analysis of the scheme is given in the subsequent sections.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Notations Throughout the paper, ‚ą•¬∑‚ą•‚ąě is defined by ‚ą•őľ‚ą•‚ąě=sup(u,v)|őľ(u,v)| denotes standard supremum norm for a function őľ defined on some domain ő∑. N and M denote the number of mesh points in space (u) and time (v) directions, respectively. c denotes a generic positive constant independent of the őĶ and the mesh sizes.

steps.title: Midpoint upwind method
steps.instruction: Applying the midpoint upwind method on the outside layer region of Eq.¬†(6) gives:(11)¬£muN,MZij+1={‚ąíőĶDu+Du‚ąíZij+1+pi‚ąí1/2Du‚ąíZij+1+Qi‚ąí1/2Zi‚ąí1/2j+1=gi‚ąí1/2j+1,Zj+1(0)=ŌÜ0j+1,j=1(1)M‚ąí1,Zj+1(1)=ŌÜ1j+1,j=1(1)M‚ąí1,where Du+Du‚ąíőėij+1=2‚Ąďi+‚Ąďi‚ąí1(őėi+1j+1‚ąíőėij+1‚Ąďi‚ąíőėij+1‚ąíőėi‚ąí1j+1‚Ąďi‚ąí1),Du‚ąíőėij+1=őėij+1‚ąíőėi‚ąí1j+1‚Ąďi, ¬£muN,Mőėij+1 is the midpoint upwind difference operator, pi‚ąí1/2=(pi+pi‚ąí12), Qi‚ąí1/2=(Qi+Qi‚ąí12) and gi‚ąí1/2j+1=(gij+1+gi‚ąí1j+12).

steps.title: Midpoint upwind method
steps.instruction: Eq.¬†(11) can be written in the form of systems of equations¬†as:(12)¬£muN,MZij+1=ŌĎi‚ąíZi‚ąí1j+1+ŌĎi0Zij+1+ŌĎi+Zi+1j+1=Gij+1,i=1(1)N‚ąí1,j=0(1)M‚ąí1.whereŌĎi‚ąí=‚ąí2őĶ‚Ąďi‚ąí1(‚Ąďi+‚Ąďi‚ąí1)‚ąípi‚ąí1/2‚Ąďi+Qi‚ąí1/22,ŌĎi0=2őĶ‚Ąďi‚Ąďi‚ąí1+pi‚ąí1/2‚Ąďi+Qi‚ąí1/22,ŌĎi+=‚ąí2őĶ‚Ąďi(‚Ąďi+‚Ąďi‚ąí1),Gij+1=gi‚ąí1/2j+1.

steps.title: Cubic spline in tension method
steps.instruction: We discritized the inside layer region of Eq.¬†(10) via cubic spline in tension method as discussed below. A function Sj+1(u,ŌĀ)‚ąąC2[0,1] which interpolates Zj+1(u) at the mesh points ui, i=0(1)N, depends on a parameter ŌĀ>0 reduces to cubic spline in the interval [0,1] as ŌĀ‚Üí0 is known as parametric cubic spline function. The spline function Sj+1(u,ŌĀ)=Sj+1(u) satisfying in [ui,ui+1], the differential equation(13)d2Sj+1(u)d2u+ŌĀSj+1(u)=[d2Sj+1(ui)du2+ŌĀSj+1(ui)](ui+1‚ąíu‚Ąď)+[d2Sj+1(ui+1)du2+ŌĀSj+1(ui+1)](u‚ąíui‚Ąď),where Sj+1(ui)=Zij+1 and ŌĀ>0 is known to be cubic spline in tension. Using the result in [27], we obtain the following scheme:(14)ŌÉ1‚ĄďiMi+1j+1+ŌÉ2(‚Ąďi+‚Ąďi‚ąí1)Mij+1+ŌÉ1‚Ąďi‚ąí1Mi‚ąí1j+1=Zi+1j+1‚ąíZij+1‚Ąďi‚ąíZij+1‚ąíZi‚ąí1j+1‚Ąďi‚ąí1,fori=1(1)N‚ąí1,where ŌÉ1=1ŌÉ2(1‚ąíŌÉsinhŌÉ),ŌÉ2=1ŌÉ2(ŌÉcothŌÉ‚ąí1), ŌÉ=‚ĄďŌĀ1/2 and Mk=d2Sj+1(uk)du2,k=i,i¬Ī1. For the choice of parameters ŌÉ1+ŌÉ2=1/2, Eq.¬†(14) is stable and has potential for tackling the given differential problem.

steps.title: Error analysis
steps.instruction: őĶ‚ąíuniform convergence analysis of the proposed numerical scheme is the primary focus of this section. We analyzed the őĶ‚ąíuniform convergence of the proposed method divided into two cases as follows.

steps.title: Error analysis
steps.instruction: Case (i). For i=N/2+1(1)N, we have the truncation error
